label: Reactor No. 4 — Explosion Site
sub: Site of the April 26, 1986 explosion. Now covered by the New Safe Confinement arch (2016), housing approximately 200 tonnes of corium nuclear fuel.

label: Pripyat — Abandoned City
sub: Soviet model city of 49,360 residents, evacuated 36 hours after the explosion on April 27, 1986. Now a ghost city within the exclusion zone, slowly being reclaimed by forest.

label: Chernobyl City
sub: The city of Chernobyl (population ~12,000 in 1986) was evacuated in May 1986 and remains within the exclusion zone. It now serves as the administrative hub for exclusion zone workers.

label: Red Forest — Highest Radiation Zone
sub: The pine forest 500 metres west of the reactor turned rust-red after the explosion from extreme radiation. Bulldozed and buried in trenches in 1986; soil remains among the most radioactively contaminated on Earth.

label: New Safe Confinement
sub: The 257-metre-wide stainless steel arch completed November 2016, built over the original 1986 sarcophagus. Funded by 45+ countries at a cost of €2.1 billion; designed to last 100 years.

label: Kopachi — Buried Village
sub: A village whose highly contaminated buildings were demolished and buried underground in 1986. Only a kindergarten was left standing as a memorial. The burial created permanent underground radioactive waste.

label: Slavutych — Replacement City
sub: Built in 1986–1988 to replace Pripyat, Slavutych houses workers from the Chernobyl NPP. Located outside the exclusion zone, it was constructed by 8 Soviet republics, each building its own residential quarter.

label: Kyiv — Radiation Monitoring
sub: Ukrainian capital 130 km south of Chernobyl. Received elevated radiation levels in late April 1986; the May Day parade proceeded despite this, a symbol of Soviet denial. Now home to the National Chernobyl Museum.

label: Minsk — Belarus Contamination Hub
sub: Capital of Belarus, which received approximately 23% of total Chernobyl fallout — more than Ukraine or Russia. The Belarusian government established the most extensive radioactive contamination monitoring network in the former USSR.

label: Forsmark — Sweden Detects Fallout
sub: Sweden's Forsmark Nuclear Power Plant on April 28, 1986, where workers' contaminated clothing triggered alarms, forcing the Soviet Union to acknowledge the disaster. This was the first public revelation of the Chernobyl accident internationally.

label: Vienna — IAEA Headquarters
sub: The International Atomic Energy Agency hosted the critical August 1986 post-accident review meeting where Soviet scientists presented their analysis of the disaster, and where the INSAG-7 report was later developed.

label: National Chernobyl Museum, Kyiv
sub: Opened in 1992, the National Chernobyl Museum in Kyiv preserves the history of the disaster through photographs, artifacts, and personal testimonies. A major memorial and educational institution visited by thousands annually.

label: 30 km Exclusion Zone Boundary
sub: The Chernobyl Exclusion Zone established in May 1986 extends approximately 30 km in all directions from the plant, covering 2,600 km². Permanent habitation is prohibited. Wildlife has returned in large numbers.

label: Prypiat River — Contamination Pathway
sub: The Prypiat River, flowing through the exclusion zone into the Kyiv Reservoir, was a major pathway for radioactive contamination. Sand bags were placed along banks to prevent runoff; the river remains monitored for cesium-137 and strontium-90.

label: Kyiv Reservoir — Water Contamination Risk
sub: The Kyiv Reservoir, supplying drinking water to Ukraine's capital, was at risk of contamination from radioactive runoff. Monitoring programs were established immediately after the disaster and continue today.

label: Gomel Region — Highest Belarus Contamination
sub: The Gomel region of Belarus received some of the highest deposition of cesium-137 and other radionuclides outside the immediate Chernobyl zone. Significant areas remain in the Belarusian exclusion zone and restricted zones today.

label: Chernobyl NPP Administration
sub: The administrative building of the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant, from which plant director Viktor Bryukhanov initially reported the accident as a minor event. Now the operational center for decommissioning activities.

label: Alley of Remembrance, Chernobyl City
sub: Memorial alley in Chernobyl city commemorating the liquidators and victims of the disaster. Includes a memorial to the 31 official deaths and plaques for the towns and villages within the exclusion zone.

label: Poliske — Contaminated Town
sub: The town of Poliske in Kyiv Oblast was evacuated in 1986 as part of the expanded exclusion zone. Located approximately 90 km from the reactor, it received significant contamination and remains within Ukraine's exclusion zone.

label: Central Exclusion Zone Monitoring Station
sub: One of numerous radiation monitoring stations operated by the Ukrainian State Emergency Service within the exclusion zone. Continuously monitors air, soil, water, and biological samples for radioactive isotopes.
